text:
Behind a collapsed wall, on a pedestal that has clearly eaten hands before: {relic}. It's real. It's here. The stories all end badly, but they do all mention it's worth a fortune.

The argument around "Dungeon Artifact Find" ends wherever someone finally admits that the border follows a ruler's old argument and cuts directly through a community's kitchen.

outcomes.text: The relic wakes at your touch and tastes the curse already riding you. 'Oh good,' it seems to sigh, 'someone pre-ruined.' It comes along without a single trap firing. Professional courtesy.
Your fingers close around it. Nothing bites. Somewhere, several factions just felt a disturbance in their ledgers.
The pedestal WAS a mimic. You take the relic anyway, mid-wrestle, because you have your priorities straight.

success.text: Three tripwires, a pressure plate, and a dart hole shaped like a disappointed face. You defeat them all with patience.

fail.text: The dart mechanism clicks, swivels to aim, takes in the state of you, and lowers itself. Even traps have standards. You limp off with the relic, unshot.
You find the trap by triggering it. The dart's note reads 'told you.' You grab the relic while bleeding.

choices.text: Leave it. Some things should stay legendary

outcomes.text: You walk away. The pedestal seems almost disappointed. Your restraint feels valuable, which is good, because it's all you're leaving with.

choices.text: Swap in your lucky rock, heist-style

outcomes.text: You place the rock as you lift the relic, one smooth motion, no daylight between them. The pedestal accepts the exchange. It may even prefer the rock; the rock, at least, will stay.

text:
The entrance door bears a carved sign: 'WELCOME. NO REFUNDS. MIND THE GAP.' There is, as far as you can see, no gap.

Officials classify "Dungeon No Refunds Door" as routine, although a merchant has found a way to own the risk while renting it back to everyone in danger.

outcomes.text: You stride in, and your own glow finds the gap before your feet do: a neat black slot in the floor, annotated by physics. You step over it, radiantly smug. The dark is wasted on you.

Your companion, walking ahead and mid-sentence, finds the gap with one leg. You haul them out by the collar. They insist on adding 'THE GAP IS REAL' to the sign in charcoal before allowing the journey to continue.
There was a gap. You find it with your face. The dungeon's sense of humor is structural.
You step over where a gap would be, on a hunch. Somewhere, a trap sighs in disappointment.

success.text: The stick finds the gap, then a second gap, then a mimic pretending to be a third gap. You proceed safely, stickless.

fail.text: The stick was the mimic. It bites your hand and scampers off, offended.

outcomes.text: A voice from inside: 'It's open! Mind the gap!' Forewarned is forearmed. Whoever they are, you never find them.

text:
A sword is wedged in a rib cage. The plaque reads: 'HERE LIES THE PREVIOUS OWNER OF THIS SWORD.' The sword looks excellent.

People remember "Dungeon Ribcage Sword" differently, but the material fact remains: a mapmaker has moved the border again without consulting the houses underneath the ink.

outcomes.text: The skeleton's sockets fix on the artifact you carry, and its jaw drops in what can only be respect. The fingers unclench from the hilt, one by one. Between looters, apparently, there is honor.
The sword starts its whisper, tastes the curse already on you, and goes quiet, like a salesman realizing the customer is a colleague. It comes free without a word of complaint.
Nothing happens. It's just a great sword and a very honest plaque.
The rib cage's original owner objects, briefly and skeletally. You keep the sword, but at a cost.
The sword whispers 'finally' in a voice like a satisfied tax auditor. You are now, apparently, in a partnership.

choices.text: Add a coin to the grave and move on

outcomes.text: The skeleton's jaw creaks into what might be a smile. Somewhere ahead, a trap quietly disarms itself.

success.text: '...ESTATE SALE SATURDAY.' You come back Saturday. The estate includes a modest bag of gold.

fail.text: The fine print is in Skeletal. You nod like you understood. The rib cage nods back. You leave quickly.

text:
A goblin sits at a tiny desk before a bridge. 'Toll bridge,' he says. 'Five gold. I don't make the rules.' He gestures at a rock labeled RULES.

Nobody affected by "Dungeon Goblin Toll" was consulted. The part authority avoids is that the ledger reports excellent health and requests that nobody examine the patient.

outcomes.text: The goblin checks a list, finds your name on the GOOD side of it, and waves you through free. 'Friends of the region ride free. The rock insists.' The rock says nothing, benevolently.
The goblin squints at you. 'You've got the look of someone who's paid a LOT of tolls.' He stamps your hand at the veteran rate and notes it on the rock, which now bears an asterisk.
He stamps your hand with a mushroom. 'Receipt.' The bridge is sturdy and the transaction weirdly professional.

success.text: You argue jurisdiction. The goblin consults the rock, loses the argument on your behalf, and waves you through, muttering about precedent.

fail.text: The rock wins. Somehow. There's a surcharge now for 'contempt of rock.'

success.text: You anchor your rope to a boulder and cross hand over hand, boots barely damp. The goblin holds up a slate reading 7/10. The rock holds up nothing, but you can tell it's impressed.
Cold, wet, free. The goblin watches you the whole way, taking notes for a fee he'll invent later.

fail.text: The river takes you, but the rope takes you back. You come up coughing, still holding the line, dignity waterlogged but attached.
The river is deeper, colder, and more opinionated than expected. You emerge downstream with less dignity and one fewer boot.

choices.text: Offer jerky in lieu of coin

outcomes.text: The goblin sniffs the jerky, consults the rock, and declares it legal tender. 'Bridge policy accepts snacks.' He waves you across mid-chew and stamps your hand twice, for flavor.

text:
An altar to a forgotten god. The offering bowl holds three coins, a button, and a note: 'PLEASE. ANYTHING. I USED TO HAVE A WHOLE THING.'

A plaque may eventually misdescribe "Dungeon Forgotten Altar". For now, the useful truth is that faith has arrived as practical courage; authority has arrived dressed like faith.

outcomes.text: The coins vanish. The god, starving for any work at all, notices the curse on you the way a stray notices a sandwich. There is a sensation like being licked by warm static, and then the curse is simply gone. 'TIP JAR IS THE BOWL,' adds the dark, hopefully.
Warmth floods the room. A voice, rusty with disuse: 'A CUSTOMER.' You are blessed with the enthusiasm of the recently remembered.
The god overdoes the blessing, badly out of practice. Your hair will be golden for a month whether you like it or not.

Your hand shakes over the bowl. The dark watches you sway, and something like a sigh moves through the room. 'OH. OH NO. YOU'RE WORSE OFF THAN I AM. TAKE THEM. GET SOMETHING WARM.' Divine pity: still technically a blessing.
Nothing happens. The god can't afford consequences right now.
A whisper follows you for the rest of the day: 'rude. RUDE.' Your luck feels thinner.

choices.text: Leave the button. Take nothing. Say something kind

outcomes.text: The dark goes very still around the artifact you carry. 'I REMEMBER THAT,' the god whispers. 'FROM WHEN I HAD A WHOLE THING.' You hold it up and let the god look, for old times' sake. Reverence flows the wrong direction for a while, and some of it sticks to you.
'...thank you,' says the dark, and means it. You leave feeling inexplicably taller.

text:
A gelatinous cube fills the corridor, drifting slowly, absorbing bones and debris. A sign taped to it reads: 'CLEANING IN PROGRESS. PLEASE USE OTHER HALL.' There is no other hall.

Officials classify "Dungeon Slime Janitorial" as routine, although the people maintaining this place know exactly how it fails and are forbidden to say so near customers.

outcomes.text: Your companion, bored by minute six, starts tossing pebbles for the cube. The cube absorbs each one with what can only be called enthusiasm, then extrudes a small pre-cleaned treasure like a tip. You have taught a gelatinous cube to play fetch. History will not record this, but you will.
Twenty minutes of respectful silence. The cube passes, leaving the corridor immaculate. You could eat off this floor. Something clearly has.
While waiting, you notice a coin purse suspended in the cube, mid-digestion. You fish it out with a stick and quick hands. Finders keepers; the cube doesn't argue.

success.text: You limbo along the wall, inches from digestion. The cube ripples, possibly in appreciation of your form.

fail.text: It got your sleeve. Just the sleeve. The dissolving noise will feature in your dreams, but the arm is fine.

outcomes.text: The cube stops. Quivers. In eight hundred years, no one has ever. It extrudes a small, perfectly cleaned trinket from its interior: a tip, or a gift, or a hostage. You take it either way.

text:
A door with four dials and an inscription: 'SPEAK THE ANSWER: WHAT WALKS ON FOUR LEGS AT DAWN, TWO AT NOON, AND THREE AT DUSK?' Below, scratched by a previous visitor: 'ITS NOT MAN. WE TRIED MAN.'

Nobody affected by "Dungeon Puzzle Door" was consulted. The part authority avoids is that the instrument is precise, the theory is provisional, and the sponsor wants a slogan.

success.text: 'A dog. A dog that learned to beg, then hurt its paw.' The door considers this, decides it respects you, and opens. Inside: the previous riddler's abandoned supplies.

fail.text: You say 'man' anyway, with confidence. The door administers a corrective jolt. The scratched warning gains a tally mark, courtesy of your elbow.

outcomes.text: It was never locked. The riddle was decorative. Somewhere, an architect is still laughing.
The handle was the trap. The riddle was the distraction. Your hand is the lesson.
You and your companion perform the riddle as a two-hander, complete with a dramatic pause and a wagon noise. The door opens halfway through, which in theater terms is a standing ovation.
You pose one about a merchant, a wagon, and three crates labeled DO NOT ASK. The door thinks for a full minute, then opens out of intellectual courtesy. Doors get bored too.

choices.text: Skip the riddle. Pick the lock

outcomes.text: The lock yields in under a minute. The door mutters something about the death of scholarly discourse, but rules are rules: open is open. The riddler's abandoned pack sits just inside, still packed.

text:
You interrupt a circle of chests, barrels, and one apologetic bookshelf arranged in a ring. A banner droops overhead: 'DISGUISE-DEPENDENT PREDATORS ANONYMOUS. ALL FORMS WELCOME.'

Nobody affected by "Dungeon Mimic Support Group" was consulted. The part authority avoids is that the world is terrible at rewarding decency and strangely dependent upon it.

outcomes.text: The circle senses the curse on you and goes reverently quiet. 'A lived-experience speaker,' whispers the bookshelf. You share. A barrel takes notes. You leave with the group's blessing, a commemorative token, and the rare warmth of being understood by furniture.
A barrel shares its fear of being opened 'for the wrong reasons.' You cry a little. Everyone pretends not to notice, which for mimics is deeply on-brand. They gift you a member's token.

choices.text: Ask which of them is the real treasure chest

outcomes.text: Every lid in the circle swivels toward the artifact you carry. Actual treasure. In the room. Unattended-adjacent. The group's collective willpower fails as one, and in the ensuing scramble of splinters and shame, coins scatter everywhere. You leave with a share and no one in a position to judge you.
A hurt silence. 'We're ALL real,' says the bookshelf, quivering. You have set the group back weeks and are asked to leave.
One chest, new to the group and weak of will, cracks open to prove itself. Gold spills. The group sighs. Relapse.

choices.text: Share your own struggles with pretending

outcomes.text: They know the ballads. Hearing the person FROM the ballads admit the smile is fake breaks the whole circle open. Even the banner droops lower, moved. They insist you take provisions and a token of honorary membership. You are, officially, a mimic now: something pretending to be a treasure.
You talk about smiling through tavern ballads about your life. The circle is moved. A chest dabs its lid. You leave lighter, with the group's blessing and a snack for the road.
